title: AdaPower: Specializing World Foundation Models for Predictive Manipulation
authors: Yuhang Huang; Shilong Zou; Jiazhao Zhang; Xinwang Liu; Ruizhen Hu; Kai Xu
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv v1, 3 Dec 2025
open_source_notes: No code repository or released weights were identified in the supplied paper text.
primary_problem: World Modeling; Planning; Test-Time Adaptation
world_model_detail: Cosmos-Predict2-2B Diffusion Transformer adapted with TS-TTT, Memory Persistence, and an action encoder
planning_representation: Observation-level imagined trajectories plus candidate action sequences
horizon_basis: Evaluates extended rollouts on unseen LIBERO-LONG tasks; an exact numeric MPC horizon was not identified.
learning_type: Foundation Model Adaptation; Self-supervised Test-Time Training
pretraining: Yes
observation_modality: RGB video; action history
action_space: Continuous
benchmark: LIBERO-90; LIBERO-LONG / 10 unseen LIBERO tasks; custom real-world tasks
main_contribution: Adapts a general video world foundation model into a control-oriented specialist model that evaluates VLA-proposed action sequences inside MPC.
novelty: Combines temporal-spatial test-time training with persistent cross-attention memory while keeping the pretrained WFM frozen.
limitations: Introduces moderate test-time overhead; broader domain transfer and real-time efficiency remain unresolved.
future_work: Improve domain-transfer robustness, real-time efficiency, and deployment in broader intelligent systems.
evidence_world_model: The paper replaces the WFM text encoder with an action encoder and uses the adapted model to generate action-conditioned future video rollouts.
evidence_planning: A VLA proposes candidate action sequences; the specialist world model rolls them out and a reward model selects the sequence executed by MPC.
evidence_horizon: Memory Persistence is designed to reduce error accumulation, and evaluation includes long-horizon rollouts on LIBERO-LONG.
review_flags: Planning horizon is qualitative rather than numerically reported; open-source status requires external verification.
source_document: AdaPower_ Specializing World Foundation Models for Predictive Manipulation.txt

title: AdaWorldPolicy: World-Model-Driven Diffusion Policy with Online Adaptive Learning for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Ge Yuan; Qiyuan Qiao; Jing Zhang; Dong Xu
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv v1, 23 Feb 2026
open_source_notes: A project homepage is provided, but a code or weights release was not established from the supplied text.
primary_problem: Policy Learning; World Modeling; Online Adaptation
world_model_detail: Cosmos-Predict2-based world model connected to 0.4B DiT action and force experts through multimodal self-attention
prediction_target: Future observation; force feedback; action sequence
planning_method: None — model-informed diffusion policy without explicit MPC, search, or trajectory optimization
horizon_basis: The policy generates action chunks of 12–32 actions and is evaluated on long-horizon tasks, but the paper does not implement an explicit planner.
learning_type: Imitation Learning; Self-supervised Online Adaptation; Foundation Model
pretraining: Yes
observation_modality: Multimodal: static RGB; gripper RGB; force-torque; state/text conditioning
action_space: Continuous action chunks
benchmark: LIBERO-10; Variant PushT; CALVIN; custom real-world tasks
robot_platform: INOVO robotic arm; benchmark-specific simulated manipulators
main_contribution: Unifies a video world model, diffusion action expert, and force predictor so prediction error can drive lightweight online policy adaptation.
novelty: Alternates between action generation and future imagination, using world-model and force prediction errors as self-supervised test-time update signals.
limitations: The large video backbone is compute intensive and can produce blur or artifacts in cluttered scenes; long-horizon planning failures remain.
future_work: Address long-horizon planning failures and scale the adaptation mechanism to larger architectures.
evidence_world_model: The action-conditioned Cosmos-based module predicts future observations and supplies an online self-supervision signal.
evidence_planning: No separate planner is described; the DiT action expert directly generates action chunks in closed loop.
evidence_horizon: CALVIN long-sequence evaluation and a long-horizon egg pick-and-place task are reported; action horizons range from 12 to 32.
review_flags: Do not count as an explicit planning method in planner-frequency plots; open-source status requires external verification.
source_document: AdaWorldPolicy_ World-Model-Driven Diffusion Policy with Online Adaptive Learning for Robotic Manipulation.txt

title: A knowledge-based task planning approach for robot multi-task manipulation
authors: Deshuai Zheng; Jin Yan; Tao Xue; Yong Liu
year_basis: First public appearance: published online 18 Jul 2023; journal volume is dated 2024
open_source_notes: The article is open access under CC BY 4.0; a code release was not identified in the supplied text.
primary_problem: Planning
world_model_detail: N/A
horizon_basis: The planner produces short sequences of reusable primitive tasks until a stop token; no extended-horizon evaluation is reported.
learning_type: Supervised; Environment-Interactive Learning
pretraining: Yes — primitive policies
observation_modality: Language; knowledge graph
action_space: Hybrid: discrete primitive selection plus continuous primitive-policy execution
benchmark: Custom 422-task language dataset; Meta-World 20-task subset
robot_platform: Simulation-only Meta-World manipulator; specific arm not stated in the extracted evidence
main_contribution: Uses an RGCN combining GCN and LSTM to convert language-conditioned task decomposition into sequential graph-edge prediction.
novelty: Injects object-task-skill relations from a semantic knowledge graph and supports learning unseen task types through environment interaction.
limitations: Language-only planning can produce fixed decompositions that ignore object-specific variations, and transitions between adjacent primitive policies are not optimized.
future_work: Add visual features for unseen-object generalization and develop better conjunction strategies between primitive policies.
evidence_world_model: The system models semantic task and object relations rather than predicting environmental dynamics, so it is not classified as a world model.
evidence_planning: The RGCN predicts primitive tasks sequentially from an activated knowledge graph until it emits stop.
evidence_horizon: Examples and evaluation focus on compact primitive sequences; the paper does not claim long-horizon planning.
review_flags: The short-horizon label is an inference from reported primitive sequences; code-release status is unverified.

title: Causal-JEPA: Learning World Models through Object-Level Latent Masking
authors: Heejeong Nam; Quentin Le Lidec; Lucas Maes; Yann LeCun; Randall Balestriero
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2602.11389 in 2026; ICML 2026 publication
open_source_notes: Code repository reported by the paper: https://github.com/galilai-group/cjepa
primary_problem: World Modeling; Planning; Representation Learning
world_model_detail: Object-centric JEPA with a frozen visual/object encoder and a masked transformer predictor trained on history completion and future latent prediction.
prediction_target: Masked historical object states and future object-centric latent states
horizon_basis: MPC uses H=5 action blocks of size B=5, producing a 25-action optimized sequence; the evaluation episode budget is 50 steps.
learning_type: Self-supervised World Model Learning
policy_type: None — actions are selected by MPC rather than a learned policy
pretraining: Yes — frozen DINOv2-derived visual features and object-centric encoder
observation_modality: RGB video; action; proprioception
goal_representation: Goal observation encoded as an object-centric latent state
action_space: Continuous
benchmark: CLEVRER; Push-T
main_contribution: Introduces object-level latent masking in a JEPA world model to make interaction-dependent prediction necessary while greatly reducing planning token cost.
novelty: Treats masked object states as counterfactual-like observability interventions, combining object-centric abstraction with reconstruction-free joint latent prediction.
limitations: Performance is bounded by the object-centric encoder, influence neighborhoods are not validated against explicit temporal causal graphs, and evaluation is limited to relatively controlled environments.
future_work: Jointly refine stronger object-centric encoders without collapse and evaluate in more complex environments with richer interactions and explicit causal structure.
evidence_world_model: The predictor completes masked object histories and predicts future object latents, optionally conditioned on actions and proprioception.
evidence_planning: Push-T actions are optimized with CEM and repeatedly executed in an MPC scheme using predicted latent goal error.
evidence_horizon: The optimized sequence contains 25 low-level actions, which is medium under the dataset rule and is not task-level long-horizon planning.
review_flags: Core JEPA/world-model paper. Do not equate its 25-action MPC horizon with long-horizon task decomposition.
source_document: Causal-JEPA_ Learning World Models through Object-Level Latent Interventions.txt

title: Continuous control actions learning and adaptation for robotic manipulation through reinforcement learning
authors: Asad Ali Shahid; Dario Piga; Francesco Braghin; Loris Roveda
year_basis: Published online 9 Feb 2022
open_source_notes: Software repository reported by the paper: https://github.com/Asad-Shahid/Intelligent-Task-Learning
primary_problem: Policy Learning; Reinforcement Learning; Adaptation
world_model_detail: N/A — PPO and SAC are used as model-free reinforcement-learning algorithms.
horizon_basis: A closed-loop policy directly executes a single grasp-and-lift task; no explicit planner or task decomposition is used.
learning_type: Model-free Reinforcement Learning; Fine-Tuning; Sim-to-Real
pretraining: Yes — a base grasping policy initializes adaptation to modified objectives
observation_modality: State: robot proprioception; object pose; relative object-gripper position
action_space: Continuous: seven joint-velocity commands plus gripper command
benchmark: Custom MuJoCo grasping and obstacle-avoidance tasks
main_contribution: Compares PPO and SAC for continuous robotic grasp control and proposes sequential fine-tuning to adapt a learned policy to additional performance and safety objectives.
novelty: Demonstrates model-free policy adaptation with an unconstrained continuous joint-command action space and zero-shot transfer from simulation to a real Franka robot.
limitations: The approach still depends on simulated training, uses engineered state information rather than vision, and evaluates a narrow grasping task; direct real-world learning remains sample inefficient.
future_work: Study rigorous one-shot versus fine-tuning comparisons, model-based and meta-RL adaptation, dynamic targets and obstacles, human-aware safety, multiagent modular control, and visual environment updates.
evidence_world_model: The paper explicitly uses model-free PPO and SAC and does not learn predictive environment dynamics.
evidence_planning: The stochastic policy maps the current 46-dimensional state directly to continuous robot commands without search or trajectory optimization.
evidence_horizon: The evaluation is a single reach-grasp-lift behavior with additional control constraints, not a long-horizon task plan.
review_flags: Supporting model-free comparison; peripheral to core world-model and explicit-planning analyses.

title: DayDreamer: World Models for Physical Robot Learning
authors: Philipp Wu; Alejandro Escontrela; Danijar Hafner; Ken Goldberg; Pieter Abbeel
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2206.14176 on 28 Jun 2022; CoRL 2022
open_source_notes: The authors publicly release the experiment infrastructure and provide a project website.
primary_problem: World Modeling; Model-Based Reinforcement Learning; Policy Learning
world_model_detail: Dreamer recurrent state-space model with multimodal encoder, stochastic latent dynamics, observation decoder, reward model, and actor-critic behavior learning.
planning_method: Policy Rollout — actor-critic optimization from imagined latent trajectories
horizon_basis: Imagined actor-critic rollouts use H=16 steps; the critic bootstraps beyond that horizon to learn longer-term strategies.
learning_type: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning; Online Real-World Learning
pretraining: No — each task is learned online from scratch
observation_modality: Multimodal: RGB cameras; proprioception; velocity and task-specific sensors
goal_representation: Reward-defined task objective; explicit goal position for navigation
action_space: Multiple: continuous and discrete across platforms
benchmark: Custom real-world A1 walking; UR5 and XArm pick-and-place; Sphero navigation
main_contribution: Demonstrates that Dreamer can learn locomotion, manipulation, and navigation directly on four physical robots without simulators or demonstrations.
novelty: Establishes a common real-world world-model baseline across diverse robots and sensing/action configurations using the same hyperparameters.
limitations: Multi-hour hardware learning causes robot wear and possible human intervention, experiments may not expose the method's ultimate limits, and the evaluated tasks remain comparatively constrained.
future_work: Train longer, tackle more challenging tasks, and combine fast real-world world-model learning with the benefits of simulation.
evidence_world_model: An action-conditioned RSSM learns latent dynamics from replayed real-world experience and predicts trajectories used for behavior learning.
evidence_planning: The actor and critic are optimized on imagined latent rollouts rather than by deployment-time MPC or search.
evidence_horizon: The imagination horizon is 16 steps; this supports long-term control strategies but not high-level long-horizon task decomposition.
review_flags: Core world-model baseline. Count as imagination-based policy planning, not deployment-time trajectory optimization.
source_document: DayDreamer_ World Models for Physical Robot Learning.txt

title: DINO-WM: World Models on Pre-trained Visual Features enable Zero-shot Planning
authors: Gaoyue Zhou; Hengkai Pan; Yann LeCun; Lerrel Pinto
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2411.04983 on 7 Nov 2024; later published at ICML 2025
open_source_notes: Code and trained models are released through the DINO-WM project repository/site.
task_domain: General Robotics; Manipulation; Navigation; Deformable-Object Control
primary_problem: World Modeling; Planning; Zero-Shot Goal Reaching
world_model_detail: Frozen DINOv2 patch encoder with an autoregressive causal ViT transition model; the image decoder is optional and not needed for planning.
video_prediction: No — latent visual dynamics are predicted without pixel reconstruction
horizon_basis: Task-dependent action sequences are optimized with receding-horizon MPC; Push-T goals are selected to be feasible within 25 steps, and no high-level task decomposition is used.
learning_type: Offline Self-Supervised World-Model Learning
policy_type: None — actions are optimized at test time
pretraining: Yes — frozen DINOv2 visual encoder
observation_modality: RGB
action_space: Continuous; environment-dependent
benchmark: Maze / PointMaze; Wall; Reach; Push-T; Rope Manipulation; Granular Manipulation
robot_platform: Simulation agents; XArm for Rope and Granular environments
main_contribution: Learns task-agnostic offline visual dynamics in pretrained DINOv2 feature space and solves unseen visual goals through test-time action optimization.
novelty: Eliminates pixel reconstruction and task-specific rewards or policies while retaining spatial patch structure suitable for zero-shot MPC.
limitations: Requires offline state-action datasets with sufficient coverage and ground-truth actions; evaluations are simulated and action-space MPC does not provide hierarchical task reasoning.
future_work: Combine with exploration and online updates, learn from unlabelled internet video without ground-truth actions, regularize planning objectives, and integrate high-level planning with low-level control.
evidence_world_model: The action-conditioned transition model autoregressively predicts future DINOv2 patch embeddings from observation and action histories.
evidence_planning: CEM optimizes action sequences against latent goal error and MPC repeatedly replans from new observations.
evidence_horizon: The evaluated behavior is multi-step visual goal reaching, including Push-T targets feasible within 25 steps, rather than long-horizon symbolic or skill-level task planning.
review_flags: Core world-model and explicit-planning paper. Record year as 2024 under the first-public-appearance rule, despite its ICML 2025 publication.
source_document: DINO-WM_ World Models on Pre-trained Visual Features enable Zero-shot Planning.txt

title: Dream-Tac: A Unified Tactile World Action Model for Contact-Rich Robot Manipulation
authors: Yunfan Lou; Yifan Ye; Yankai Fu; Jun Cen; Xiaowei Chi; Yaoxu Lyu; Peidong Jia; Sirui Han; Zhihe Lu; Shanghang Zhang
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2606.08737 on 7 Jun 2026
open_source_notes: Code repository is released by the authors.
primary_problem: Policy Learning; World Action Modeling; Visuo-Tactile Prediction
world_model_detail: Pretrained video Diffusion Transformer with a shared visual, tactile, robot-state, and action latent space plus contact-aware self-attention.
prediction_target: Future visual observations; future tactile observations; 20-step action chunk
action_conditioned: Yes — actions and future observations are jointly generated
planning_method: None — unified generative policy without MPC, search, or trajectory optimization
horizon_basis: The model directly generates a fixed 20-step action chunk; this is policy horizon rather than an explicit planning horizon.
replanning_online: Yes — action chunks are refreshed during closed-loop execution
learning_type: Imitation Learning; Foundation-Model Fine-Tuning
pretraining: Yes — pretrained video DiT, T5 encoder, and video VAE
observation_modality: Multimodal: third-person RGB; wrist RGB; dual tactile RGB; robot state
action_space: Continuous action chunks
benchmark: Six custom real-world tasks: Pick Baguette; Insert USB; Clean Whiteboard; Cut Banana; Peel Cucumber; Play Mahjong
robot_platform: Franka Emika Panda with RealSense D435i cameras and Xense Photon tactile sensors
main_contribution: Jointly predicts actions, future visual observations, and future tactile dynamics to improve contact-rich manipulation.
novelty: Introduces contact-gated visuo-tactile fusion, a contact-aware attention bias, and diffusion-step caching for faster real-time inference.
limitations: Evaluated on a limited task and object set; the simple frame-difference contact gate may miss subtle or long-horizon interactions; diffusion inference remains expensive relative to lightweight reactive policies.
future_work: Scale visuo-tactile data, develop richer contact models, improve real-time efficiency, and extend to dexterous, deformable, multi-stage, and long-horizon contact-rich tasks.
evidence_world_model: The shared diffusion model denoises future image, future tactile, and action tokens together in one latent sequence.
evidence_planning: No separate planner is used; generated action chunks are executed as a closed-loop policy.
evidence_horizon: The action chunk is H=20, but the evaluated tasks are individual contact-rich skills rather than long-horizon task plans.
review_flags: Core world-action-model paper, but exclude from explicit planner-frequency counts.
source_document: Dream-Tac_ A Unified Tactile World Action Model for Contact-Rich Robot Manipulation.txt

title: DynaWM: A Base-VLA-Guided World Foundation Model for Moving-Object Manipulation
authors: Chongkei Chang; Zhidong Deng

primary_problem: Policy Learning; Dynamic Action Refinement; Benchmark and Dataset
language_conditioned: Yes — inherited through the frozen base VLA
world_model_detail: A V-JEPA 2.1 history encoder, Mamba-3 base-action encoder, state encoder, and flow-matching DiT regenerate action chunks; the method does not explicitly roll out future observations or states.
action_conditioned: Yes — conditioned on the frozen base VLA action chunk
planning_method: None — closed-loop action regeneration without search or model rollout
horizon_basis: DynaWM regenerates a 10-step action chunk; the paper calls this a short-horizon signal rather than an explicit plan.

goal_representation: Language instruction or subtask processed by the base VLA

main_contribution: Adds a policy-agnostic action-regeneration module that uses recent motion evidence to correct frozen VLA action chunks for moving-target interception.
novelty: Uses the physical action trajectory as a common interface across heterogeneous VLA architectures and introduces a dedicated moving-object benchmark and dataset.
limitations: Inferred from the study design: evaluation is simulation-only, depends on base-VLA proposals, uses short 10-step chunks, and does not explicitly predict or roll out future environment states.

main_contribution: Reviews embodied AI from foundational components through LLM/MLLM-driven and WM-driven systems and argues for their integration.

limitations: MLLMs lack reliable physical grounding and real-time adaptation, while world models lack open-ended semantics and generalizable task decomposition; deployment is also constrained by hardware, energy, safety, and trustworthiness.
future_work: Advance autonomous long-duration agents, adaptive multimodal perception, joint real-time MLLM-WM control, hardware-software co-design, collaborative world models for swarms, lifelong learning, and trustworthy deployment.
evidence_world_model: The survey categorizes RSSM-, JEPA-, and Transformer-based world models and emphasizes internal representations and future prediction.
evidence_planning: LLMs are described as task decomposers, while world models support imagination-based and anticipatory decision making.
evidence_horizon: The proposed research agenda explicitly targets long-duration autonomous operation, spatiotemporal memory, and prediction across multiple horizons.
review_flags: Exclude from method-only counts; retain for taxonomy, conceptual framing, and gap-analysis sections.
source_document: Embodied AI_From LLMs to World Models.txt

title: EmbodiSwap for Zero-Shot Robot Imitation Learning
authors: Eadom Dessalene; Pavan Mantripragada; Michael Maynord; Yiannis Aloimonos
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2510.03706 on 4 Oct 2025
open_source_notes: The authors release the overlay-generation code, synthetic dataset, model checkpoints, and inference code.
primary_problem: Imitation Learning; Data Generation; Cross-Embodiment Transfer
world_model_detail: V-JEPA is used as a pretrained video-predictive visual backbone, but the downstream method predicts end-effector motion rather than environment dynamics.
video_prediction: No — the downstream output is an end-effector trajectory
horizon_basis: The closed-loop policy repeatedly predicts a future end-effector transform for five individual skills; it does not optimize a multi-step plan.
replanning_online: Yes — the policy predicts a new command from each updated observation
learning_type: Imitation Learning; Self-Supervised Video Pretraining; Synthetic Data Generation
pretraining: Yes — pretrained V-JEPA encoder and predictor
observation_modality: RGB; optional proprioception and action-location tokens
goal_representation: Implicit action category learned from segmented human-video demonstrations
action_space: Continuous relative 6D end-effector transform
benchmark: EPIC-Kitchens; HOI4D; Ego4D synthetic overlays; custom real-world Open, Close, Pour, Place, and Cut tasks
simulation: No — synthetic video compositing is used instead of a physics simulator
main_contribution: Creates photorealistic robot overlays on in-the-wild human video and trains a zero-shot closed-loop manipulation policy using V-JEPA features.
novelty: Bridges the human-to-robot embodiment gap through video editing and demonstrates that V-JEPA feature-prediction pretraining transfers strongly to robot trajectory forecasting.
limitations: Failures arise from robot joint singularities, self-occlusion, and visually plausible but physically unsuccessful trajectories; the method also lacks explicit 3D object reasoning and high-level task composition.
future_work: Predict in joint space, use multiple camera perspectives, incorporate 3D object models for in-hand manipulation, and compose learned skills within higher-level cognitive frameworks.
evidence_world_model: The pretrained V-JEPA predictor provides visual features, but the trained task head forecasts robot motion rather than future environment states.
evidence_planning: At each closed-loop step, the policy directly emits a relative end-effector command without search or imagined trajectory evaluation.
evidence_horizon: Evaluation covers five single manipulation skills and does not include multi-stage or long-horizon task decomposition.
review_flags: JEPA-related but not a JEPA world-model method under the strict taxonomy; include in representation-transfer and imitation-learning analyses.

title: Enhancing the LLM-Based Robot Manipulation Through Human-Robot Collaboration

world_model_detail: N/A — environment information is supplied by object detection and depth sensing rather than a learned predictive dynamics model.
horizon_basis: GPT-4 classifies temporally extended tasks and decomposes them into subtasks, each containing multiple executable motion functions.

limitations: Performance is sensitive to object-localization error, LLM token variability, incorrect DMP calls, human intervention requirements, and compounding subtask failures.

evidence_planning: GPT-4 decomposes long-horizon commands into subtasks and then maps each subtask to executable motion functions.

title: Extended Tree Search for Robot Task and Motion Planning
authors: Tianyu Ren; Georgia Chalvatzaki; Jan Peters

planning_representation: Hybrid: symbolic task skeletons plus continuous geometric motion bindings

future_work: Advance discrete-continuous inference, factor-graph formulations for optimal control and hybrid task-and-motion planning, and integration with differentiable deep learning.

horizon_basis: The benchmark tasks last approximately 600–2300 low-level steps or 60–230 seconds, but the baseline methods are direct learned policies rather than explicit planners.

title: Hierarchical World Models as Visual Whole-Body Humanoid Controllers
authors: Nicklas Hansen; Jyothir S V; Vlad Sobal; Yann LeCun; Xiaolong Wang; Hao Su
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2405.18418 in May 2024; published at ICLR 2025
open_source_notes: Code, environments, and checkpoints are released through the Puppeteer project page.
primary_problem: World Modeling; Model-Based Reinforcement Learning; Hierarchical Control
world_model_detail: Two independently trained TD-MPC2 decoder-free latent world models: a high-level visual puppeteer and a reusable low-level motion-tracking controller.
prediction_target: Next latent state; reward; termination; terminal value
horizon_basis: Both levels use TD-MPC2 planning with H=3; the hierarchy is architectural rather than a long temporal abstraction.
learning_type: Online Model-Based Reinforcement Learning; Motion-Capture Pretraining
policy_type: Hierarchical TD-MPC2 controller with policy priors
pretraining: Yes — the low-level tracking model is pretrained on retargeted CMU motion-capture data
observation_modality: RGB; proprioception; contact sensing; abstract reference commands
goal_representation: Reward-defined locomotion task; geometric end-effector reference command
action_space: Continuous: 56-dimensional joint control at the low level and 15-dimensional geometric commands at the high level
benchmark: Custom 8-task visual whole-body humanoid-control suite: corridor, hurdles, walls, gaps, stairs, stand, walk, run
robot_platform: Simulated 56-DoF CMU Humanoid in DM Control / MuJoCo
main_contribution: Introduces a two-level latent world-model controller that converts visual task observations into reusable geometric motion commands and joint-level humanoid control.
novelty: Uses algorithmically identical TD-MPC2 world models at both hierarchy levels while learning human-like motion without hand-designed skill primitives or imitation rewards.
limitations: Evaluation is simulation-only and primarily locomotion-focused; the hierarchy has only two levels, and most experiments pretrain only the low-level model.
future_work: Broaden the task suite and study pretraining, transfer, and reuse of the complete hierarchical world model.
evidence_world_model: Each level learns an action-conditioned latent transition, reward, value, termination, and policy-prior model without reconstructing raw observations.
evidence_planning: Both the high-level command sequence and low-level joint-action sequence are optimized with TD-MPC2's MPPI-based MPC.
evidence_horizon: The planner horizon is H=3 and episodes can last 500 steps; the tasks are sustained control rather than task-level long-horizon decomposition.

title: Inner Monologue: Embodied Reasoning through Planning with Language Models
authors: Wenlong Huang; Fei Xia; Ted Xiao; Harris Chan; Jacky Liang; Pete Florence; Andy Zeng; Jonathan Tompson; Igor Mordatch; Yevgen Chebotar; Pierre Sermanet; Noah Brown; Tomas Jackson; Linda Luu; Sergey Levine; Karol Hausman; Brian Ichter
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2207.05608 in July 2022; published in the CoRL 2022 proceedings released in 2023

primary_problem: Planning; Embodied Reasoning; Closed-Loop Language Feedback
world_model_detail: N/A — the system grounds a frozen LLM with textual feedback, value functions, perception modules, and pretrained skills rather than learned environment dynamics.
horizon_basis: The LLM iteratively selects short-horizon skills for unseen multi-step tabletop and mobile-manipulation instructions.
learning_type: Few-Shot Prompting; Pretrained Skill Composition
pretraining: Yes — frozen LLMs, perception models, affordance/value functions, and low-level policies

benchmark: Simulated tabletop rearrangement; real UR5e tabletop rearrangement; real office-kitchen mobile manipulation
robot_platform: UR5e; Everyday Robots mobile manipulator; simulated tabletop robot
main_contribution: Closes the LLM planning loop by continually injecting textual success, scene, and human feedback so the robot can retry, replan, and ask clarifying questions.
novelty: Uses natural language as a common feedback interface across multiple LLM planners, perception systems, skill libraries, simulations, and real robots without additional model training.

future_work: Automate scene feedback with captioning and VQA, represent feedback uncertainty, and add safety and ethics feedback modules.
evidence_world_model: The planner reacts to textual observations but does not learn an action-conditioned predictive model of the environment.
evidence_planning: A pretrained LLM selects and sequences short-horizon skills, then incorporates success and scene feedback before producing subsequent steps.
evidence_horizon: The system evaluates unseen long-horizon sorting, navigation, drawer, and mobile-manipulation tasks that require multiple skill calls.
review_flags: Core closed-loop long-horizon language-planning paper; distinguish reactive textual feedback from learned world-model prediction.

title: IntentVLA: Short-Horizon Intent Modeling for Aliased Robot Manipulation
authors: Shijie Lian; Bin Yu; Xiaopeng Lin; Zhaolong Shen; Laurence Tianruo Yang; Yurun Jin; Haishan Liu; Changti Wu; Hang Yuan; Cong Huang; Kai Chen
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2605.14712 on 14 May 2026
open_source_notes: The manuscript links a public GitHub repository.
primary_problem: Policy Learning; Partial Observability; Short-Horizon Intent Modeling
world_model_detail: A frozen VGGT history encoder and Qwen3-VL context produce an intent-conditioned representation for a DiT flow-matching action head; no future environment state is predicted.
planning_method: None — receding-horizon policy execution without explicit search or model rollout
horizon_basis: The method stabilizes adjacent action chunks using recent visual history but does not implement a planning algorithm.
replanning_online: Yes — action chunks are regenerated from updated observations and recent history
learning_type: Imitation Learning; Flow Matching; Vision-Language Pretraining
pretraining: Yes — frozen VGGT and Qwen3-VL components

evidence_world_model: The model predicts actions conditioned on history; it does not predict future observations, latent environment states, rewards, or dynamics.

title: IQL-TD-MPC: Implicit Q-Learning for Hierarchical Model Predictive Control
authors: Rohan Chitnis; Yingchen Xu; Bobak Hashemi; Lucas Lehnert; Urun Dogan; Zheqing Zhu; Olivier Delalleau
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2306.00867 on 1 Jun 2023
publication_status: Preprint / under review in supplied manuscript
open_source_notes: A project website and video results are available, but a dedicated code release was not established from the supplied manuscript.
primary_problem: World Modeling; Offline Reinforcement Learning; Hierarchical Planning
world_model_detail: Offline TD-MPC augmented with Implicit Q-Learning; a temporally abstract manager learns a latent action-conditioned model and generates intent embeddings for an arbitrary offline-RL worker.
prediction_target: Temporally abstract future latent state; reward; value
horizon_basis: The manager plans H=4 abstract steps with temporal coarseness k=8, corresponding to 32 environment steps.
replanning_online: Yes — the manager recomputes intent embeddings using MPC during evaluation
learning_type: Offline Model-Based Reinforcement Learning; Implicit Q-Learning
pretraining: Yes — the manager is trained offline before worker training and evaluation
observation_modality: Low-dimensional environment state

action_space: Continuous environment actions for workers; discrete latent manager actions
benchmark: D4RL Maze2D; AntMaze; HalfCheetah; Walker2D; Hopper
main_contribution: Adapts TD-MPC to offline data with IQL and uses a temporally abstract model-based manager to supply latent subgoal-like intent embeddings to arbitrary offline-RL workers.
novelty: Improves multiple otherwise unchanged worker algorithms through simple state concatenation with manager-generated intent embeddings.
limitations: Hierarchy harms some fine-grained locomotion tasks, the worker does not explicitly optimize toward the intent, the abstraction timescale is fixed, and manager rollouts are computationally expensive.
future_work: Use model-based workers, learn adaptive manager timescales, understand harmful intents, and accelerate world-model rollout with more parallel architectures.
evidence_world_model: The manager learns an encoder, action-conditioned latent dynamics, reward model, critic, and policy used for abstract MPC.
evidence_planning: CEM-based MPC optimizes manager action sequences whose resulting latent intent is concatenated to the worker state.
evidence_horizon: Although H=4 is short in abstract steps, k=8 makes it a 32-environment-step medium planning horizon on long-horizon sparse-reward tasks.
review_flags: Core hierarchical world-model planning paper outside manipulation; task-level long-horizon is Yes while explicit planning_horizon is Medium.

title: JEPA-VLA: Video Predictive Embedding is Needed for VLA Models

year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2602.11832 on 12 Feb 2026
open_source_notes: No companion code repository was identified in the supplied manuscript.
primary_problem: Policy Learning; Representation Transfer; Generalization
world_model_detail: Uses frozen V-JEPA 2 video-predictive embeddings as additional VLA features, but the downstream method does not learn or roll out environment dynamics.
video_prediction: No — V-JEPA 2 was pretrained predictively, but JEPA-VLA itself performs action prediction
horizon_basis: The model generates action chunks and is evaluated on LIBERO Long tasks, but it does not implement search, MPC, or task decomposition.
replanning_online: Yes — action chunks are regenerated from current observations and recent frames
learning_type: Imitation Learning; Vision-Language Pretraining; Predictive Representation Transfer
policy_type: VLA; linear or OpenVLA-OFT action head
pretraining: Yes — frozen V-JEPA 2 and pretrained VLM/VLA backbones
observation_modality: Language; current multi-view RGB; two-frame video history; proprioception
action_space: Discrete action tokens or continuous action chunks, depending on the base VLA
benchmark: LIBERO; LIBERO-plus; RoboTwin2.0; CortexBench; custom real-world pick-and-place
robot_platform: Aloha-AgileX dual-arm simulation; Piper single-arm real robot; benchmark-specific simulated manipulators
main_contribution: Shows that video-predictive V-JEPA 2 features provide task-relevant state information and policy priors that improve VLA sample efficiency and generalization.
novelty: Introduces early-fusion and gated cross-attention mechanisms for adding predictive video embeddings to both lightly trained and large-scale pretrained VLAs.
limitations: The method uses a simple two-frame predictive representation, evaluates only one real-world task, and does not learn a task-specific dynamics model or explicit planner.
future_work: Develop more principled fusion mechanisms and further exploit large-scale video-predictive pretraining for robust, generalizable embodied policies.
evidence_world_model: A frozen V-JEPA 2 encoder supplies predictive features, while the trained downstream model directly outputs actions rather than future environment states.
evidence_planning: No model rollout or candidate action evaluation is described; predictive embeddings condition a standard VLA action model.
evidence_horizon: LIBERO Long performance is reported, but the method remains a chunked reactive policy rather than a long-horizon planner.
review_flags: JEPA-related representation-transfer paper, not a downstream JEPA world model. Include in long-horizon task evaluation but exclude from explicit-planner and world-model counts.
source_document: JEPA-VLA_ Video Predictive Embedding is Needed for VLA Models.txt

title: Learning Fine-Grained Bimanual Manipulation with Low-Cost Hardware
authors: Tony Z. Zhao; Vikash Kumar; Sergey Levine; Chelsea Finn
year_basis: Published at Robotics: Science and Systems 2023; an earlier public preprint date was not established from the supplied manuscript
open_source_notes: ALOHA hardware designs, code, datasets, and project materials are publicly released.
primary_problem: Imitation Learning; Teleoperation; Action Chunking
world_model_detail: N/A — ACT directly maps visual and proprioceptive observations to future joint-action chunks.
prediction_target: Future sequence of absolute bimanual joint positions
horizon_basis: ACT predicts overlapping action chunks to reduce effective trajectory length, but performs no search or model-based planning.
replanning_online: Yes — the policy is queried every timestep and temporally ensembles overlapping chunks
learning_type: Imitation Learning; Conditional Variational Autoencoder
pretraining: Yes — ResNet-18 visual backbones; policy trained from task demonstrations
observation_modality: Four RGB cameras; 14-DoF bimanual joint positions
goal_representation: Implicit task goal from task-specific demonstrations
action_space: Continuous 14-dimensional absolute joint-position commands
benchmark: Two simulated bimanual tasks and six real-world ALOHA tasks, including Ziploc opening, battery insertion, cup opening, Velcro threading, tape preparation, and shoe placement
robot_platform: ALOHA: two ViperX follower arms with WidowX leader arms for teleoperation
main_contribution: Introduces the low-cost ALOHA bimanual teleoperation platform and ACT, a transformer CVAE that predicts temporally ensembled action chunks.
novelty: Reduces imitation-learning compounding errors by predicting long action chunks while retaining closed-loop responsiveness through overlapping temporal ensembling.
limitations: Parallel-jaw grippers cannot perform many multi-finger or fingernail-dependent tasks, low-cost motors limit force, and ACT struggles with visually ambiguous deformable-object stages and unseen recovery states.
future_work: Not explicitly stated; the limitations motivate stronger hardware, richer sensing, more diverse data, and improved recovery and deformable-object learning.
evidence_world_model: The paper explicitly favors a learned closed-loop policy over modeling complex contact and deformation dynamics.
evidence_planning: ACT directly generates k-step joint commands and averages overlapping predictions; it does not evaluate candidate trajectories through a model.
evidence_horizon: Real demonstrations span roughly 400–700 control steps, but each benchmark is an individual fine-manipulation skill rather than task-level long-horizon planning.
review_flags: Important action-chunking and hardware baseline; exclude from world-model and explicit-planning counts.
source_document: Learning Fine-Grained Bimanual Manipulation with Low-Cost Hardware.txt

title: Learning Latent Graph Dynamics for Visual Manipulation of Deformable Objects
authors: Xiao Ma; David Hsu; Wee Sun Lee
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2104.12149 in April 2021; supplied revision dated 5 Mar 2022
open_source_notes: A demonstration video is provided, but no code repository was identified in the supplied manuscript.
primary_problem: World Modeling; Planning; Deformable-Object Control
world_model_detail: G-DOOM learns recurrent graph dynamics over unsupervised visual keypoints and tracks a history-dependent belief to handle self-occlusion.
prediction_target: Future keypoint features, recurrent belief state, and manipulation reward
horizon_basis: Evaluation permits action sequences of up to 20 steps and repeatedly applies graph-based MPC.
learning_type: Self-Supervised Representation Learning; Contrastive Dynamics Learning
pretraining: Yes — unsupervised Transporter keypoint extractor before graph-dynamics training
observation_modality: Top-down masked depth images
goal_representation: Goal image or learned reward objective
action_space: Continuous planar keypoint-centered manipulation actions
benchmark: Rope straightening; cloth flattening; cloth folding in Nvidia Flex / SoftGym
robot_platform: Kinova Gen3 with Robotiq gripper and Kinect 2.0 depth camera
main_contribution: Learns a sparse recurrent graph world model over visual keypoints and uses it for real-time MPC on rope and cloth manipulation.
novelty: Combines unsupervised keypoint discovery, recurrent graph belief tracking, contrastive dynamics learning, and keypoint-guided CEM search.
limitations: The evaluated dynamics are relatively smooth and simple; harder tasks such as knot tying or complex garment folding require larger datasets, stronger dynamics models, and longer-horizon planning.
future_work: Scale dynamics learning and planning to complex deformable-object tasks requiring richer data and long-horizon reasoning.
evidence_world_model: An action-conditioned recurrent GNN predicts the evolution of a sparse keypoint-based object representation.
evidence_planning: Graph-based MPC initializes candidate action sequences around detected keypoints and selects actions using predicted cumulative reward.
evidence_horizon: The maximum evaluation sequence is 20 steps, which is medium under the dataset rule and not task-level long-horizon planning.
review_flags: Core structured latent world-model and MPC paper; useful for object-centric and deformable-manipulation analyses.
source_document: Learning Latent Graph Dynamics for Deformable Object Manipulation..txt

title: Learning Robot Manipulation from Audio World Models
authors: Fan Zhang; Michael Gienger
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2512.08405 on 9 Dec 2025
open_source_notes: No code or project repository was identified in the supplied manuscript.
task_domain: Manipulation; Audio-Visual Robot Learning; Dexterous Control
primary_problem: World Modeling; Policy Learning; Multimodal Prediction
world_model_detail: A transformer latent flow-matching model predicts future audio spectrogram or MIDI representations, which are decoded and supplied to a separate robot policy.
planning_method: None — predicted audio is used as lookahead context for a direct policy
horizon_basis: The world model predicts approximately 2.56 seconds of audio for water filling or 8 seconds of MIDI, while the downstream policies directly output actions.
replanning_online: Yes — water-filling predictions and actions are refreshed in closed loop; music is extended autoregressively
learning_type: Generative Flow Matching; Imitation Learning; Reinforcement Learning
policy_type: Flow-Matching Manipulation Policy; Soft Actor-Critic for piano playing
pretraining: Yes — AudioMAE or MusicVAE audio representations
observation_modality: Audio spectrogram or MIDI; RGB image for water filling
goal_representation: Predicted future audio dynamics or generated future note goals
action_space: Continuous 6-DoF end-effector velocity chunks; simulated piano joint actions
benchmark: Custom real-world water filling; RoboPianist-style simulated piano duet on two songs
robot_platform: Kinova Gen3 real robot; simulated piano-playing dexterous agent
main_contribution: Introduces an audio world model that predicts physically informative future sound and uses those predictions to improve robot policies when vision is ambiguous.
novelty: Treats pitch and rhythm as predictable environment dynamics rather than merely adding audio as an auxiliary current observation.
limitations: The evaluation contains only two specialized tasks, the audio world model is action-free, and generated futures guide a policy without explicit counterfactual action evaluation.
future_work: Extend audio world modeling to more complex, fine-grained, and dexterous manipulation tasks.
evidence_world_model: Latent flow matching predicts future audio states that encode filling progress or upcoming musical structure.
evidence_planning: The predicted audio is concatenated into a flow-matching or SAC policy; no candidate action search or world-model-conditioned MPC is used.
evidence_horizon: Future audio spans seconds, but the tasks are individual skills and the action policy horizon is 16 steps rather than task-level long-horizon planning.
review_flags: Core multimodal world-model paper, but exclude from explicit-planner counts because the model supplies predictive context to a direct policy.
source_document: Learning Robot Manipulation from Audio World Models.txt

title: Long-Horizon Multi-Robot Rearrangement Planning for Construction Assembly
authors: Valentin N. Hartmann; Andreas Orthey; Danny Driess; Ozgur S. Oguz; Marc Toussaint
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2106.02489 in Jun 2021; supplied revision dated 7 Mar 2022
open_source_notes: The experiments use the publicly available RAI framework, but a paper-specific code release was not established from the supplied manuscript.
task_domain: Construction Assembly; Multi-Robot Manipulation; Task and Motion Planning
primary_problem: Planning; Multi-Robot Coordination; Construction Assembly
world_model_detail: N/A — the method uses explicit symbolic, geometric, and space-time planning models rather than learned dynamics.
planning_method: Hierarchical Task and Motion Planning; Optimization; Bi-directional Space-Time RRT
planning_representation: Hybrid: symbolic task assignments, manipulation constraints, and continuous space-time trajectories
horizon_basis: The planner solves construction sequences with up to 113 placed parts and 12 heterogeneous agents by composing limited-horizon subproblems.
replanning_online: No — the authors state that the current system is too slow for online replanning
learning_type: None — optimization and sampling-based planning
pretraining: No
observation_modality: Structured architectural model; robot and object configurations; manipulation constraints
goal_representation: Target assembly geometry and object goal poses
action_space: Hybrid: discrete task assignments and continuous multi-robot trajectories
benchmark: Tower (15 parts); wall (36); well (52); pavilion (113); handover; real six-box stacking
robot_platform: Simulated mobile manipulator, mobile KUKA arm, and crane; two real Panda arms
main_contribution: Decomposes long-horizon multi-robot construction TAMP into tractable subproblems and coordinates asynchronous agents through space-time planning.
novelty: Combines time-embedded keyframe optimization with a bidirectional space-time RRT that plans to unknown arrival times while respecting previously fixed robot trajectories.
limitations: Planning is too slow for online replanning; the formulation assumes monotonic rearrangement, light objects, known start and goal poses, and controlled execution without fastening or structural-support modeling.
future_work: Improve keyframe generation and multi-query planning, and incorporate spatial and temporal execution uncertainty for real construction deployment.
evidence_world_model: The method uses logic-geometric programming and explicit collision and manipulation constraints rather than learning environment dynamics.
evidence_planning: Limited-horizon task assignments, joint manipulation optimization, and bidirectional space-time RRT paths are combined into a complete assembly plan.
evidence_horizon: Experiments scale to 113 parts and 12 agents, and the paper explicitly targets long-horizon multi-robot assembly.
review_flags: Core classical long-horizon multi-robot TAMP paper; highly relevant as a non-learned planning baseline.
source_document: Long-Horizon Multi-Robot Rearrangement Planning for Construction Assembly.txt

title: Maintenance Automation: Methods for Robotics Manipulation Planning and Execution
authors: Christian Friedrich; Ralf Gulde; Armin Lechler; Alexander Verl
year_basis: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering in May 2022
venue: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
open_source_notes: The system uses ROS and a public CANopen stack, but a complete paper-specific implementation was not identified.
task_domain: Industrial Manipulation; Maintenance; Assembly and Disassembly
primary_problem: Planning; Execution; Maintenance Automation
world_model_detail: N/A — the system uses CAD, RGB-D perception, symbolic relations, and explicit robot-control models.
planning_method: Hierarchical Symbolic Planning; Sampling-Based Path Planning; Runtime Skill Decomposition
planning_representation: Hybrid: symbolic component relations, geometric paths, and executable skill primitives
horizon_basis: Real tasks last roughly 271–544 seconds; the valve example expands into 29 disassembly skill steps followed by reassembly.
replanning_online: Yes — the runtime interpreter adapts skill execution and can select an alternative plan after failure, although robust alternative-plan generation remains an open problem
learning_type: None — symbolic, geometric, and rule-based planning
policy_type: Reactive Skill-Primitive Library with hybrid control
pretraining: No
observation_modality: CAD; RGB-D; force-torque sensing; robot and object state
action_space: Hybrid: manipulation primitives, Cartesian velocity commands, tool actions, and position/force/visual-servo control
benchmark: Peg-in-hole; panel assembly; lubricant exchange; valve disassembly and reassembly
robot_platform: Schunk LWA4p with Schunk FTM 75 force-torque sensor and Kinect v2
main_contribution: Integrates task planning, environment modeling, path planning, symbolic-plan interpretation, and hybrid control into a complete autonomous maintenance system.
novelty: Transforms general symbolic manipulation primitives into sensor-aware executable skill sequences at runtime, bridging planning and practical robot control.
limitations: The system currently supports linear monotonic plans and one arm; success is limited by sensing, controller speed, tooling, wear, corrosion, and insufficient recovery strategies.
future_work: Extend to dual-arm manipulation, non-monotonic subassemblies, alternative-plan generation, and more reliable execution under component wear and corrosion.
evidence_world_model: Current environment state is fused from CAD and sensors, but no learned predictive transition model is used.
evidence_planning: A task planner produces symbolic manipulation primitives, a path planner supplies trajectories, and a runtime interpreter expands them into controlled skills.
evidence_horizon: The four real applications require multi-stage autonomous sequences lasting several minutes, including complete disassembly and reassembly.
review_flags: Core industrial long-horizon planning-and-execution system; not a learned world model.

title: Mem-World: Memory-Augmented Action-Conditioned World Models for Persistent Robot Manipulation
authors: Zirui Zheng; Jiaqian Yu; Xiongfeng Peng; Jun Shi; Mingyi Li; Chao Zhang; Weiming Li; Dong Wang; Huchuan Lu; Xu Jia
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2606.18960 in Jun 2026
open_source_notes: No public code repository was identified in the supplied manuscript.
task_domain: Manipulation; Robot World Simulation; Policy Evaluation
primary_problem: World Modeling; Long-Horizon Memory; Policy Evaluation and Data Generation
world_model_detail: A Ctrl-World-based multi-view action-conditioned video model augmented with W-VMem, a temporally aware 4D surfel-indexed wrist-view memory.
planning_method: None — used for policy evaluation and synthetic-data generation rather than action search
horizon_basis: The model autoregressively generates 20-step, 20-second rollouts, but does not optimize candidate action sequences.
replanning_online: No — it rolls out supplied policy or replay action chunks
learning_type: Generative Video World Modeling; Temporal-Attention Fine-Tuning
pretraining: Yes — built on a pretrained Ctrl-World video world model
observation_modality: Three RGB views: one wrist camera and two third-person cameras; robot actions and camera geometry
goal_representation: Task-specific action sequence or policy rollout
action_space: Continuous robot action chunks supplied by a policy or replay trajectory
benchmark: DROID replay consistency evaluation; real-world policy evaluation; three long-horizon policy-improvement tasks
robot_platform: Franka Emika Panda with two external cameras and one wrist camera
simulation: No — the learned world model acts as a simulator, but training and evaluation use real robot data
main_contribution: Adds geometry-aware, temporally indexed memory to an action-conditioned multi-view video world model to preserve objects across occlusion and wrist-camera motion.
novelty: Retrieves non-redundant historical wrist observations by rendering dynamic surfels from future action-derived camera viewpoints.
limitations: Rollouts depend on an informative initial wrist view and do not enforce physical contact constraints, so visually plausible but physically impossible interactions can be generated.
future_work: Improve cross-view persistence and add physics-aware signals such as contact and force to increase rollout fidelity.
evidence_world_model: Given current and retrieved historical observations plus future actions, the model generates subsequent multi-view observations and updates its memory autoregressively.
evidence_planning: The world model evaluates supplied policies and generates post-training data; it does not conduct MPC, search, or candidate-plan selection.
evidence_horizon: The method targets persistent long-horizon rollouts and improves policy success from 58% to 72% on three long-horizon tasks.
review_flags: Core long-horizon video world model, but exclude from explicit-planning counts.
source_document: Mem-World_ Memory-Augmented Action-Conditioned World Models for Persistent Robot Manipulation.txt

title: Mesh-based Dynamics with Occlusion Reasoning for Cloth Manipulation
authors: Zixuan Huang; Xingyu Lin; David Held
year_basis: Published at Robotics: Science and Systems 2022
open_source_notes: A project website with videos and visualizations is provided, but a code release was not established from the supplied paper.
primary_problem: World Modeling; State Estimation; Planning
world_model_detail: MEDOR reconstructs the full cloth mesh, refines it with self-supervised test-time optimization, and predicts mesh dynamics with a graph neural network.
prediction_target: Future cloth mesh vertex dynamics and task reward
horizon_basis: At every decision, 500 candidate pick-and-place actions are rolled out for a horizon of one action.
replanning_online: Yes — the cloth mesh is re-estimated and a new action is selected after each pick-and-place
learning_type: Supervised Mesh Reconstruction; Self-Supervised Test-Time Optimization; Dynamics Learning
policy_type: None — action selected through model-based random shooting
pretraining: Yes — reconstruction and dynamics models are trained in simulation
observation_modality: Single-view RGB-D / depth observation
goal_representation: Flattening reward or category-specific canonical cloth pose
action_space: Continuous planar pick-and-place action
benchmark: SoftGym/CLOTH3D cloth flattening and canonicalization across trousers, shirts, dresses, skirts, and jumpsuits
robot_platform: Franka Emika Panda with Azure Kinect
main_contribution: Reconstructs occluded cloth geometry and plans with a learned full-mesh dynamics model for cloth smoothing and canonicalization.
novelty: Combines full-mesh occlusion reasoning with self-supervised test-time refinement before model-based action selection.
limitations: Reconstruction, test-time refinement, and planning are computationally expensive; real failures arise from cloth deformation during grasping and grasping the wrong number of layers.
future_work: Not explicitly stated; the reported limitations motivate faster planning, improved reconstruction, and more reliable cloth-layer grasping.
evidence_world_model: A learned GNN predicts cloth-mesh evolution under pick-and-place actions.
evidence_planning: The system randomly samples 500 candidate actions, rolls each through the mesh dynamics model, and executes the highest-reward action.
evidence_horizon: The explicit planner horizon is one pick-and-place action, although real episodes may use up to ten actions.
review_flags: Core structured world-model and MPC paper; classify planning horizon as Short, not by total episode length.
source_document: Mesh-based Dynamics with Occlusion Reasoning for Cloth Manipulation.txt

title: MimicPlay: Long-Horizon Imitation Learning by Watching Human Play
authors: Chen Wang; Linxi Fan; Jiankai Sun; Ruohan Zhang; Li Fei-Fei; Danfei Xu; Yuke Zhu; Anima Anandkumar
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2302.12422 in Feb 2023; published at CoRL 2023
open_source_notes: Code and videos are released through the MimicPlay project website.
primary_problem: Hierarchical Imitation Learning; Planning; Learning from Human Video
world_model_detail: N/A — the high-level model predicts latent 3D hand-trajectory plans, not environment transitions.
prediction_target: Goal-conditioned 3D hand trajectory and robot actions
horizon_basis: Evaluation tasks span 2,000–4,000 control steps, 100–200 seconds, and at least three subgoals.
replanning_online: Yes — the latent planner and controller operate in closed loop at approximately 17 Hz
learning_type: Imitation Learning from Human Play and Teleoperated Robot Demonstrations
pretraining: Yes — the high-level latent planner is trained first on human play and then frozen for low-level policy training
observation_modality: Multi-view human RGB video; robot current image; wrist RGB; proprioception
goal_representation: Goal image or human/robot task video prompt
action_space: Continuous 6-DoF end-effector and gripper commands
benchmark: Fourteen real-world tasks across kitchen, study desk, flower, whiteboard, sandwich, and cloth environments
simulation: Yes — supplementary simulation experiments; primary evaluation is real-world
main_contribution: Learns high-level latent plans from inexpensive human play video and uses a small amount of robot teleoperation data to train long-horizon low-level control.
novelty: Separates data sources by hierarchy level and bridges human and robot embodiments through goal-conditioned 3D hand-trajectory latent plans.
limitations: The planner is scene-specific, tasks are tabletop-only, and cross-embodiment representation learning remains limited.
future_work: Scale to internet video, mobile manipulation, temporal contrastive learning, and cycle-consistent cross-embodiment representations.
evidence_world_model: The high-level network predicts human-motion plans and the low-level policy predicts robot actions; neither predicts environment dynamics.
evidence_planning: A goal-conditioned high-level latent planner guides a separate low-level visuomotor controller throughout execution.
evidence_horizon: The paper evaluates fourteen multi-subgoal real tasks lasting 100–200 seconds and 2,000–4,000 action steps.
review_flags: Core learned hierarchical long-horizon planner, but not a world model or search-based planner.
source_document: MimicPlay_ Long-Horizon Imitation Learning by Watching Human Play.txt

title: MIND-V: Hierarchical World Model for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation with RL-based Physical Alignment
authors: Ruicheng Zhang; Mingyang Zhang; Jun Zhou; Xiaofan Liu; Zunnan Xu; Zhizhou Zhong; Puxin Yan; Haocheng Luo; Xiu Li
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2512.06628 in Dec 2025; supplied revision v4 dated 5 Jul 2026
open_source_notes: No public implementation or model release was identified in the supplied manuscript.
task_domain: Long-Horizon Manipulation; Video World Simulation; Synthetic Data Generation
primary_problem: World Modeling; Hierarchical Planning; Video Generation; Policy Learning
world_model_detail: A Gemini-based Semantic Reasoning Hub plans subtasks, a Behavioral Semantic Bridge grounds them into masks and trajectories, and a CogVideoX-based diffusion generator synthesizes video; V-JEPA 2 supplies a physical-coherence reward.
action_conditioned: No / Indirect — conditioned on planned affordances, masks, and trajectories rather than low-level robot actions
planning_method: Language Planner; Hierarchical; Visual Rollout Selection
planning_representation: Symbolic subtask tuples; affordance masks; trajectories; generated video rollouts
horizon_basis: Long-horizon evaluations contain two to four subtasks, while the autoregressive subtask design supports arbitrarily long sequences.
replanning_online: Yes — staged visual future rollouts select or refine candidate continuations at each subtask transition
learning_type: Supervised Fine-Tuning; GRPO Reinforcement Learning; Foundation-Model Adaptation
pretraining: Yes — Gemini 2.5 Pro, Affordance-R1, CogVideoX-5B, and V-JEPA 2
observation_modality: RGB scene image; language; object and robot masks; planned trajectories
goal_representation: Natural-language instruction decomposed into ordered subtasks
action_space: Structured affordance and trajectory conditions for video generation
benchmark: Bridge V2 and unseen web scenes; MimicGen Coffee, StackThree, and Square policy-learning tasks
robot_platform: Bridge V2 embodiments and MimicGen simulated manipulators
main_contribution: Introduces a hierarchical video world model that autonomously converts high-level instructions into physically aligned, long-horizon manipulation-video rollouts.
novelty: Combines VLM task decomposition, structured semantic-to-pixel conditioning, V-JEPA-based physical-reward alignment, and propose-verify-refine visual rollouts.
limitations: Staged rollout selection adds substantial inference overhead, and errors in upstream affordance localization can propagate through all downstream generation stages.
future_work: Improve inference efficiency, affordance grounding robustness, and physical fidelity while extending generated data to broader real robotic domains.
evidence_world_model: The Motor Video Generator predicts future manipulation videos, while V-JEPA 2 evaluates their latent physical coherence.
evidence_planning: The Semantic Reasoning Hub decomposes tasks and generates multiple candidate continuations that are evaluated and refined at subtask boundaries.
evidence_horizon: Long-horizon tasks contain two to four linked subtasks, and 111-frame three-subtask videos are generated autoregressively.
review_flags: Core hierarchical video world model. Its planner operates in synthetic video space and does not directly output executable robot actions. Record year as 2025 despite the supplied 2026 revision.
source_document: MIND-V_ Hierarchical Video Generation for Long-Horizon Robotic Manipulation with RL-based Physical Alignment.txt

title: Multi-robot geometric task-and-motion planning for collaborative manipulation tasks
authors: Hejia Zhang; Shao-Hung Chan; Jie Zhong; Jiaoyang Li; Peter Kolapo; Sven Koenig; Zach Agioutantis; Steven Schafrik; Stefanos Nikolaidis
year_basis: Published online 30 Oct 2023
open_source_notes: No paper-specific code release was identified in the supplied article.
task_domain: Multi-Robot Manipulation; Geometric Task and Motion Planning; Mining Robotics
primary_problem: Planning; Multi-Robot Collaboration; Rearrangement
world_model_detail: N/A — uses explicit reachability, occlusion, task-skeleton, and motion-planning models rather than learned dynamics.
planning_method: MCTS; Mixed-Integer Programming; Geometric Task and Motion Planning
planning_representation: Hybrid: collaborative manipulation graph, symbolic task skeleton, object placements, and robot trajectories
horizon_basis: Reported benchmark plans generally contain roughly three to five synchronized task stages, with each stage grounded into multi-robot motion; roof-bolting executions last 250–270 seconds.
learning_type: None — search, optimization, and motion planning
pretraining: No
observation_modality: Fully observed structured geometry, object regions, reachability, and occlusion relations
goal_representation: Goal regions for target objects and task-specific mining objectives
action_space: Hybrid: robot-object assignments, relocation and handover actions, object placements, and continuous trajectories
benchmark: Pick-and-place arms domain; mobile-manipulator box-moving domain; two underground roof-bolting scenarios
robot_platform: Multiple robotic arms and mobile manipulators; ABB arm coordinated with an autonomous roof bolter
main_contribution: Builds collaborative manipulation graphs and uses MIP-guided MCTS to find efficient multi-robot geometric task-and-motion plans.
novelty: Precomputes robot-specific reachability and occlusion information to generate promising collaborative task skeletons, including handovers, before grounding their continuous parameters.
limitations: Assumes monotone object motion, predefined handover regions, synchronous actions, and full observability; it does not optimize trajectory length or execution time directly.
future_work: Support non-monotone tasks, discover handover regions dynamically, account for sensing uncertainty and trajectory cost, use learning to accelerate planning, and extend to broader MR-TAMP problems.
evidence_world_model: All environmental reasoning uses explicit geometric and symbolic structures, not a learned predictive transition model.
evidence_planning: MIPs propose task skeletons, MCTS balances exploration and exploitation, and motion planners ground object placements and robot trajectories.
evidence_horizon: The reported task skeletons are multi-stage but relatively compact; the paper is not explicitly framed as a long-horizon planning method.
review_flags: Core multi-robot TAMP baseline. Keep separate from the explicitly long-horizon 113-part construction planner in batch 7.
source_document: Multi-robot geometric task-and-motion planning for collaborative manipulation tasks.txt

title: Octo: An Open-Source Generalist Robot Policy
authors: Octo Model Team (Dibya Ghosh; Homer Walke; Karl Pertsch; Kevin Black; Oier Mees; et al.)
year_basis: Published at Robotics: Science and Systems 2024
open_source_notes: Training code, finetuning code, model checkpoints, and data infrastructure are publicly released.
task_domain: Manipulation; Generalist Robot Policies; Cross-Embodiment Learning
primary_problem: Policy Learning; Foundation Model; Efficient Finetuning
world_model_detail: N/A — Octo directly maps task and observation tokens to future action chunks.
horizon_basis: Octo is tested on long-horizon tasks such as Stanford Coffee, but no explicit search, world-model rollout, or high-level decomposition is used.
replanning_online: Yes — the policy repeatedly generates action chunks from updated observations
learning_type: Cross-Embodiment Imitation Learning; Diffusion Action Modeling; Foundation-Policy Pretraining
policy_type: Generalist Robot Policy; Diffusion Action Policy
pretraining: Yes — trained on 800,000 Open X-Embodiment trajectories
observation_modality: Multiple RGB cameras; proprioception; optional new modalities such as force-torque after finetuning
action_space: Flexible end-effector, joint-position, single-arm, and bimanual action spaces
benchmark: Nine real robot setups including WidowX BridgeV2, UR5 Tabletop, CMU Baking, Stanford Coffee, Berkeley Insertion, Pick-Up, Coke, and Bimanual
robot_platform: Nine real robot setups across four institutions
main_contribution: Provides a fully open-source transformer policy that supports multiple robots, observations, task specifications, and action spaces and can be efficiently finetuned.
novelty: Combines a flexible token-based transformer, language or goal-image conditioning, diffusion action chunks, and adapter-based finetuning across new robot interfaces.
limitations: Performance is limited by sparse wrist-camera and language annotations, degrades for novel skills and scenes, and is trained only from optimal demonstration data on manipulation platforms.
future_work: Improve wrist-camera and language conditioning, learn from suboptimal or online interaction data, and expand to navigation and mobile manipulation.
evidence_world_model: The model predicts robot actions directly and contains no learned transition or future-observation model.

authors: Danny Driess; Fei Xia; Mehdi S. M. Sajjadi; Corey Lynch; Aakanksha Chowdhery; Brian Ichter; Ayzaan Wahid; Jonathan Tompson; Quan Vuong; Tianhe Yu; Wenlong Huang; Yevgen Chebotar; Pierre Sermanet; Daniel Duckworth; Sergey Levine; Vincent Vanhoucke; Karol Hausman; Marc Toussaint; Klaus Greff; Andy Zeng; Igor Mordatch; Pete Florence

title: PIVOT-R: Primitive-Driven Waypoint-Aware World Model for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Kaidong Zhang; Pengzhen Ren; Bingqian Lin; Junfan Lin; Shikui Ma; Hang Xu; Xiaodan Liang
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2410.10394 in Oct 2024; published at NeurIPS 2024
venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)
open_source_notes: A project website is provided, but a public code release was not established from the supplied manuscript.
primary_problem: World Modeling; Policy Learning; Waypoint Prediction; Efficient Execution
world_model_detail: A VLM parses one of ten manipulation primitives, and a scene-prediction transformer predicts the CLIP feature of a task-relevant future waypoint for conditioning a lightweight action decoder.
action_conditioned: Yes — conditioned on current observations, robot state, action history, and primitive prompt
planning_method: None — primitive parsing and waypoint prediction feed a direct action policy without search or candidate rollout evaluation
horizon_basis: The method predicts the next task-relevant waypoint and low-level action; SeaWave levels measure instruction complexity rather than temporal horizon.
hierarchical_planning: No — modules run at different frequencies, but no separate task-level plan is optimized
replanning_online: Yes — primitive, waypoint, and action predictions are refreshed asynchronously from current observations
learning_type: Imitation Learning; Latent Waypoint Prediction; Vision-Language Pretraining
pretraining: Yes — pretrained VLM and CLIP encoders
observation_modality: First- and third-person RGB; language; robot state; action history
goal_representation: Natural-language instruction parsed into manipulation primitives
action_space: Discretized delta position, delta Euler rotation, and binary gripper state
benchmark: SeaWave four-level instruction benchmark; three custom real-world tasks
robot_platform: SeaWave simulated manipulator; real robot platform not specified in the extracted manuscript text
main_contribution: Predicts sparse task-relevant visual waypoints rather than modeling every timestep, improving manipulation accuracy and execution efficiency.
novelty: Combines primitive-guided latent waypoint prediction with an asynchronous hierarchy whose modules run at different frequencies.
limitations: High-level primitive instructions and executed low-level actions can disagree, particularly for directional commands, and large action modules may overfit.
future_work: Strengthen consistency between high-level instructions and low-level execution so actions can adapt reliably to requested changes.
evidence_world_model: The scene module predicts the latent CLIP representation of a future waypoint state from current multimodal context.
evidence_planning: No search, MPC, or multi-candidate rollout stage is used; the predicted waypoint directly conditions action decoding.
evidence_horizon: The benchmark contains individual manipulation skills with progressively harder language grounding, not long-horizon multi-stage tasks.
review_flags: Core predictive waypoint world model, but exclude from explicit-planner counts. Do not interpret SeaWave Level 4 as long-horizon.
source_document: PIVOT-R_ Primitive-Driven Waypoint-Aware World Model for Robotic Manipulation.txt

limitations: The field requires large datasets and faces unexpected error sources, nonlinear and time-varying material behavior, limited real-time computation, and relatively little unsupervised learning.
future_work: Develop efficient embedded and real-time learning, better transfer and generalization, multimodal soft sensing, robust lifetime and degradation prediction, and richer simulation-to-hardware validation.
evidence_world_model: Some reviewed studies learn soft-body dynamics, but the article does not propose a unified predictive world model.
evidence_planning: Motion planning and reinforcement learning are reviewed as applications within a much broader soft-robot learning taxonomy.
evidence_horizon: No paper-level planning horizon is defined.
review_flags: Peripheral to the core world-model and long-horizon manipulation corpus; retain for deformable and soft-robot context.
source_document: Review of machine learning methods in soft robotics.txt

title: VLA-JEPA: Enhancing Vision-Language-Action Model with Latent World Model
authors: Jingwen Sun; Wenyao Zhang; Zekun Qi; Shaojie Ren; Zezhi Liu; Hanxin Zhu; Guangzhong Sun; Xin Jin; Zhibo Chen
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2602.10098 in Feb 2026; supplied version dated 17 Feb 2026
open_source_notes: Code, project page, and Hugging Face models are publicly released.
primary_problem: World Modeling; Policy Learning; Human-Video Pretraining; Generalization
world_model_detail: A frozen V-JEPA 2 world-state encoder and causal transformer predictor learn leakage-free latent state transitions conditioned on learned latent-action tokens; a flow-matching head generates robot actions.
action_conditioned: Yes — conditioned on learned latent-action tokens rather than explicit low-level actions during human-video pretraining
video_prediction: No — future states are predicted in V-JEPA latent space
planning_method: None — latent dynamics supervise a direct VLA action policy
horizon_basis: The model predicts an action chunk and latent future-state sequence; it does not search over candidate action sequences. LIBERO includes long-horizon evaluations.
replanning_online: Yes — action trajectories are regenerated from current observations during closed-loop execution
learning_type: Self-Supervised Video Pretraining; Imitation Learning; Flow Matching
policy_type: VLA with JEPA Latent World Model and Flow-Matching Action Head
pretraining: Yes — Qwen3-VL, V-JEPA 2, Something-Something-v2 human video, and DROID robot demonstrations
observation_modality: Language; multi-view RGB video; robot demonstration trajectories
action_space: Continuous action trajectories generated by conditional flow matching
benchmark: LIBERO; LIBERO-Plus; SimplerEnv; three custom real-world tasks
robot_platform: Franka Emika Panda; Google Robot; WidowX
main_contribution: Introduces leakage-free JEPA pretraining that learns action-relevant latent transition semantics from action-free human video and robot demonstrations.
novelty: Future frames are used only as latent supervision targets, avoiding pixel shortcuts and future-information leakage while reducing prior latent-action pipelines to two stages.
limitations: Training uses substantial compute, the real-world evaluation contains only three tasks and 100 demonstrations, performance is sensitive to prediction horizon, and the model provides no explicit planning or counterfactual action search.
future_work: Scale human-video pretraining with more robot data and text-based reasoning signals and improve robustness and generalization.
evidence_world_model: A causal transformer predicts future V-JEPA world-state embeddings from past states and learned latent-action tokens.
evidence_planning: The predicted latent dynamics condition a flow-matching action head; no MPC, search, or candidate-rollout scoring is used.
evidence_horizon: LIBERO long-horizon tasks are evaluated, but the method remains a closed-loop chunked policy with planning_horizon=None.
review_flags: Core JEPA/VLA world-model paper. Count as a strict latent world model but exclude from explicit-planner counts.
source_document: VLA-JEPA_ Enhancing Vision-Language-Action Model with Latent World Model.txt

year_basis: First published as a Review in Advance on 2 Dec 2024; journal volume year is 2025
